systems.name: Chord Formula and Family System
systems.summary: The central organizing system of Chord Chemistry. Every chord is defined by a formula — a specific combination of scale-degree numbers derived from the major scale built on the same root. Three chord families (MAJOR, MINOR, DOMINANT 7TH) serve as parent categories from which all extensions, altered chords, and suspended chords descend. The same-string inversion method generates exactly N distinct voicing forms for any N-note chord on a fixed string set.

traversal_rules.name: Same-String Inversion Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Move between voicing forms by raising each tone on the same string to the next higher chord tone; yields exactly N forms for an N-note chord.

references.quote_excerpt: RAISE each tone in the chord ON THE SAME STRING
any 3 note chord (triad) has only 3 different FORMS on the same set of strings

traversal_rules.name: Family Parent-to-Extension Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Navigate from parent chord to extensions/altered forms by adding or modifying tones.

references.quote_excerpt: MAJOR, MINOR, and DOMINANT 7TH chords are the 'fathers' of the 3 families
altered chords are not listed here but they mean just what they say

modification_rules.name: Altered Fifth Retention Rule
modification_rules.summary: An altered 5th must be kept in any voicing; the root may be omitted but the altered 5th may not.

references.quote_excerpt: if the 5th is altered you may not leave it out as it is essential

modification_rules.name: Root or Fifth Omission in Five-Note Chords
modification_rules.summary: In any five-note chord, the root or 5th may be omitted and one remaining tone doubled.

references.quote_excerpt: in any chord with 5 tones in the formula, the root OR 5th is often omitted

modification_rules.name: Third Omission in 11th Chords
modification_rules.summary: The 3rd is frequently omitted in 11th chords; optionally replaced by a 2nd or 4th in any chord.

references.quote_excerpt: In chords that have the 11th tone, the 3rd is often omitted

modification_rules.name: #9 and +11 Voice Placement Rule
modification_rules.summary: #9 placed above the 3rd; +11 placed above the 5th when a 5th is present.

references.quote_excerpt: the #9 is usually put above the 3rd and the +11 is usually put above the 5th

modification_rules.name: Polychord Bottom Triad Fifth Omission
modification_rules.summary: Bottom triad of a polychord may omit its 5th; upper triads stay high when bassist plays root.

references.quote_excerpt: In many cases the bottom triad may leave out the 5th
stick to medium to very high notes and let the bass player's note carry the bottom

modification_rules.name: Dominant-for-Major Substitution in Rock
modification_rules.summary: In rock contexts, dominant 7th chords and their extensions may substitute for major chords.

references.quote_excerpt: for a rock sound, quite often for a major chord you can substitute the dominant 7th and its extensions

systems.name: Voice Leading and Systematic Thinking System
systems.summary: Greene's primary methodology for connecting chords: each chord tone is treated as an independent voice, and the motion of each voice from one chord to the next is analyzed and optimized. V7–I is the foundational laboratory.

traversal_rules.name: Leading-Tone Resolution (V7 to I)
traversal_rules.summary: The 3rd of V7 moves up by half-step to the root of I.

references.quote_excerpt: The 3rd of the V7 chord moves up to the root of the I chord

traversal_rules.name: Common-Tone Smoothing
traversal_rules.summary: Retaining shared tones across adjacent chords smooths transitions.

references.quote_excerpt: common tones on top of some of these chords. This is one aid to smooth chord resolutions
COMMON TONES on the top notes will sometimes help smooth out the transitions

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Approach Chord Motion
traversal_rules.summary: A chord a half-step above or below the target is acceptable when supported by good voice leading.

references.quote_excerpt: Using a chord 1/2 step above or below the next chord is acceptable if good voice leading

traversal_rules.name: ii-V-I Cycle Motion
traversal_rules.summary: A m7 followed by dominant a 4th higher is the standard ii-V approach.

references.quote_excerpt: following a minor 7th type chord a dominant chord whose root is a 4th higher
smooth transition can be achieved if the new key is preceded by a IIm7 V7 progression

traversal_rules.name: m(maj7) to m7 Voice-Leading Step
traversal_rules.summary: Major-7 tone in m(maj7) moves one fret up or down into a root or b7 tone.

references.quote_excerpt: the 7 TONE SHOULD MOVE UP 1 FRET or DOWN 1 FRET INTO A ROOT OR b7 TONE

traversal_rules.name: Back-Cycling Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Precede any chord with its V7 (one step) or V and V-of-V (two steps) for voice-leading momentum.

references.quote_excerpt: putting in V chords right before another chord will be called back-cycling
if you go back 2 chords from any chord, you get the V and the V OF THE V

traversal_rules.name: Dominant 3-Fret Cycle Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Any dominant chord may move in 3-fret intervals as long as the final chord resolves correctly.

references.quote_excerpt: Almost any dominant type chord can be moved in intervals of 3 frets

traversal_rules.name: Altered Dominant 2-Fret Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Dominants with 5ths/#5ths may move in 2-fret intervals while still resolving.

references.quote_excerpt: Dominant chords that have 5ths and/or #5ths may be used in 2 FRET INTERVALS effectively

modification_rules.name: Fifth Omission When Voice Leading Is Strong
modification_rules.summary: The 5th of a I chord may be omitted when the voice leading is strong.

references.quote_excerpt: leaving out the 5th in a I chord is acceptable

modification_rules.name: Major 7th from Double-Root Lowering
modification_rules.summary: Lowering one root in a major chord with two roots yields a major 7th chord.

references.quote_excerpt: if you lower ONE root in a major chord that has TWO roots, you will get a major 7th chord

modification_rules.name: Melody-on-Top Requirement (Chord Melody)
modification_rules.summary: In chord melody, the melody must always sit above the chord voicing.

references.quote_excerpt: the melody is always played ABOVE the chord

modification_rules.name: No Altered I7 at Song Opening (Except 7#9)
modification_rules.summary: Altered I7 chords should not open a song; 7#9 is the only exception.

references.topic: Don't start a song with altered I7
references.quote_excerpt: you couldn't always replace a I7 at the beginning of a song with an altered chord
the +11 does not assume a tonic function

modification_rules.name: Lowering 3rd and b7 to Generate IV9 (Rock)
modification_rules.summary: Taking any dominant and lowering its 3rd and b7 by one fret each produces a 9th chord on the IV.

references.quote_excerpt: if you take any 7th chord and lower the 3rd and 7th 1 fret each, you will get a 9th chord built on the IV
you may progressively lower the 3rd and b7th, one fret at a time

systems.name: Substitution and Cycle-of-Fourths System
systems.summary: How chords are substituted for one another and how harmonic motion through the cycle of 4ths/5ths creates momentum and modulation. Dominant 7 is the engine; tritone substitution is the master rule.

traversal_rules.name: Tritone Substitution Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Any dominant may be replaced by the dominant a tritone away.

references.quote_excerpt: all dominant chords a 5th apart are related
use chords a b5th higher than those in the cycle if you like the sound

traversal_rules.name: Cycle-of-Fourths Dominant Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Dominants move through the cycle of 4ths/5ths; each resolves to a chord a 4th higher.

references.quote_excerpt: One key for understanding these patterns is the cycle of 4ths
When dominant chords are used as the V7 of the key or THE V7 of a V7

traversal_rules.name: Duration-Splitting ii-V Interpolation
traversal_rules.summary: Play a m7 a 5th above for part of a dominant's duration, then resolve to the dominant.

references.quote_excerpt: you may play a m7 type chord whose root is a 5th higher than the dominant

traversal_rules.name: Half-Bar Dominant Insertion
traversal_rules.summary: When a chord is followed by another a 4th higher, split first chord and insert dominant on its root for second half.

references.quote_excerpt: you may divide the duration of the 1st chord in 1/2 and play a dominant chord with the same root

traversal_rules.name: vii° as Rootless V7 Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: The diminished triad on the 7th degree functions as a rootless V7.

references.quote_excerpt: VII diminished triad sounds like a dominant 7th chord whose root is 2 whole steps lower

traversal_rules.name: Chord Synonym Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: Chords with identical tones are interchangeable.

references.quote_excerpt: knowing chord synonyms is that you can use them to come up with substitute chords

traversal_rules.name: Scale-Fragment Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: A fragment of diatonic harmonies may substitute for a single chord; the ending chord must align with the replaced one.

references.quote_excerpt: widely used concept of playing a fragment of the fundamental harmonies of a scale
the starting chord is not that important, but the ending chord is very important

traversal_rules.name: Resolving Diminished Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: Three notes of a diminished 7 one fret above V7's root combined with tonic root replaces V7.

references.quote_excerpt: any 3 notes of a diminished 7th chord whose root is one fret higher than the V7
This chord will now replace the V7 chord, and will be called a RESOLVING DIMINISHED CHORD

traversal_rules.name: Diminished Passing Chord Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Diminished 7 chords (every 3 frets) and #IVo pass between structural chords.

references.quote_excerpt: Diminished chords are used as 'passing' chords usually
any form of the diminished chord repeats itself every 3 frets
often a #IV° is used as a passing chord following a IV or IV7 type chord

modification_rules.name: m6 Avoidance in ii-V Context
modification_rules.summary: m6 should not be used on a minor chord followed by a dominant a 4th higher.

references.quote_excerpt: Minor 6th type chords usually should not be used for a minor chord that is followed by a dominant chord a 4th higher

modification_rules.name: Altered Minor Restriction to m7b5
modification_rules.summary: The only widely used altered minor chord is m7b5.

references.quote_excerpt: The only altered minor chord that is widely used is the m7b5 type

modification_rules.name: 11b9 Reserved for Authentic V7 Function
modification_rules.summary: 11b9 is used only for V7 of a key.

references.quote_excerpt: The 11b9 chords are usually used for the V7 chord of a key, but not often for others

modification_rules.name: Turnaround Must Cadence on V7
modification_rules.summary: Last chord of a turnaround must be V7 type or its b5 voicing.

references.quote_excerpt: The last chord of a turnaround is almost always a V7 type or its b5

modification_rules.name: Dominant Sus as Partial or Full V7 Replacement
modification_rules.summary: Sus dominants may share duration with V7 or stand alone as a full substitute when unresolved.

references.quote_excerpt: Dominant suspended chords are usually used as partial replacements for dominant 7th chords
Sometimes suspended chords are left unresolved

modification_rules.name: 9b5 Preferred Over +11
modification_rules.summary: 9b5 is more common than +11 as altered dominant; less muddy sound.

references.quote_excerpt: It is actually more common to play a 9b5 than a +11
9b5's have a less muddy sound and are more easily used

systems.name: Fingerboard Geography and Navigation System
systems.summary: The foundational spatial system mapping theoretical knowledge onto the physical guitar neck via two sets of reference points and 1st/6th string symmetry.

members.name: Open Strings (E A D G B E)
1st Reference Points (5th Fret / 4th Fret for String 3)

traversal_rules.name: Two-Reference-Point Navigation
traversal_rules.summary: Find any note by identifying which reference point is alphabetically closer and counting from it.

references.quote_excerpt: ask yourself if F# is closer to A or E in the alphabet
on the 5th fret are the same as the open string notes on the next higher string

traversal_rules.name: 1st/6th String Symmetry Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Notes on the 6th string apply identically to the 1st string at the same fret.

references.quote_excerpt: notes on the 1st and 6th strings are always the same letter names on identical frets

traversal_rules.name: Moveable Shape Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Any non-open shape transposes to any key by sliding frets.

references.topic: chord chart key choice and transposition
references.quote_excerpt: they are all moveable to other keys; to play a C6 you could raise an A6 3 frets

traversal_rules.name: Physical Proximity Chord Grouping
traversal_rules.summary: Memorize voicings by spatial clusters; learn physically related chords together.

references.quote_excerpt: try to look for chords that are closely related physically
If you can relate to chords in this manner, it

modification_rules.name: Augmented 4-Fret Symmetry (Three Names)
modification_rules.summary: Augmented chords repeat every 4 frets; each form has three enharmonic names.

references.quote_excerpt: Augmented chords repeat every 4 frets
Augmented chords repeat themselves every 4 frets the same way diminished chords repeat every 3 frets

modification_rules.name: Diminished 3-Fret Symmetry (Four Names)
modification_rules.summary: Diminished 7 chords repeat every 3 frets; each form has four enharmonic names.

references.quote_excerpt: any form of the diminished chord repeats itself every 3 frets
